title: What compostable packaging actually means
subtitle: When it actually composts and when it goes to landfill anyway
excerpt: Packaging labelled compostable falls into two categories: home-compostable (breaks down in a garden bin within 6-12 weeks) and industrially compostable (requires a commercial facility at 60 degrees C and does not break down in a home bin). Most compostable packaging on UK shelves is the industrial category.
sourceNotes: WRAP compostable packaging guidance for consumers (2023). European Bioplastics EN 13432 and EN 14045 certification standards. WRAP/Green Alliance compostable plastics in UK waste streams report (2022).

glossaryTerms.definition: Packaging certified to EN 13432 that breaks down completely within 12 weeks at 60 degrees C in a commercial composting facility. It does not break down in a home compost heap and should not be placed in one. Look for the Seedling logo (OK Compost Industrial).

Packaging certified to EN 13432 and additionally tested to OK Compost HOME or TUV AUSTRIA HOME criteria, confirming breakdown in unmanaged conditions at ambient temperature. Can go into a home compost bin. Usually certified by the seedling logo with additional HOME text.

content.text: The word compostable on packaging does not tell you what to do with it. The distinction that matters is whether it is
. The majority of compostable packaging in UK supermarkets is industrially compostable.
The problem with industrial-only compostable packaging

Industrial compostable packaging requires a commercial composting facility at high temperature. The UK has limited capacity for this. Most council food waste goes to anaerobic digestion plants, which handle organic food waste but not packaging films. Compostable packaging placed in food waste bins either disrupts the AD process or passes through undigested and goes to landfill.
What to do with each type

Home-compostable packaging (OK Compost HOME certification): add to the home compost bin. In a hot heap it will break down within two to three weeks. In a cold heap it takes six to twelve weeks. Small items like uncoated paper tea bags and plant-fibre nets will break down in any heap.

Industrially compostable packaging with no local facility: general waste bin. Placing it in recycling contaminates the plastic stream. It looks like conventional plastic but does not work in standard recycling. Placing it in food waste will likely result in landfill.

Industrially compostable packaging in an area with a dedicated collection: check the local authority waste guidance by material type. This is rare, but some councils are trialling dedicated collections.
Seedling logo only (green logo with seedling): industrially compostable (EN 13432).
Seedling logo with HOME text: home compostable (EN 14045 or OK Compost HOME).
No certification mark: no regulatory claim. The word compostable without a certification mark is an unsubstantiated claim under the Green Claims Code and should be treated as unverified.

Compostable packaging still has a manufacturing carbon cost even when it composts correctly. Reducing packaging in the first place, and choosing goods with recyclable or reusable packaging, comes before relying on compostability to solve the end-of-life problem.
